data.title: Medical Properties Trust: Recent Asset Sales Prove The Shorts Are Wrong
data.description:
Medical Properties Trust (NYSE:MPW) is in recovery mode and Mr. Market is having a hard time catching up.

As many of you know by now, I am a Medical Properties Trust Bull (in the short- to medium term). I've written extensively on this ticker and my thesis hasn't changed. Medical Properties Trust is in recovery mode. Its portfolio is stabilizing and operational performance is getting better and better. In order to prevent myself from publishing articles by rehashing stuff from my previous articles, I won't do it this time as well.

In this article I will focus on the Prospect Medical Holdings story that is now really closing and I will provide a different valuation perspective on the entire portfolio, using a recent sale that MPW put through.

When the Q3 2025 report hits the market, I will go over all the numbers and provide my next take on this interesting and very turbulent REIT.

For new readers it is important that this article is another write-up. If you are interested in my entire view of this company, it's best to check my previous articles on Medical Properties Trust. In this article I will give (new) readers some directions to certain relevant and informative chapters from some of my articles.

Without further ado, let us begin!

'Prospect Medical Holdings' is Officially in the Rearview Mirror

As many of you know by now, NOR Healthcare Systems won the bid for the six California hospitals, previously leased to Prospect. Besides the fact that Prospect wasn't paying rent to begin with, some bears have tried to scare investors by saying that NOR was "created" just a few months prior to winning the bid. But as I showed readers back in September, the current CEO (Mike Sarian) is an experienced healthcare manager that left Prospect began extracting capital from the hospitals. To counter the bear arguments that "NOR healthcare is shady and Mr. Sarian worked at Prospect" even further, you could even reason that Mr. Sarian didn't agree with the + $650 million in dividends that Prospect paid to its owners between 2012 and 2018, and decided to leave the company all together.

But that's a bit speculative, so let's get back to the fact. NOR Healthcare won the bid on the California hospitals and these were:

  • Bellflower Behavioral Health Hospital
  • Los Angeles Community Hospital
  • Norwalk Community Hospital
  • Southern California Hospital (Culver City)
  • Southern California Hospital (Hollywood)
  • Van Nuys Behavioral Health Hospital

What's left on the properties list are the following hospitals:

  • Manchester Memorial Hospital
  • Rockville General Hospital
  • Waterbury Hospital

On September 29 news came out regarding Prospect Medical Holdings. First of all, $105 million in debtor-in-possession is expected to be fully repaid by Prospect to MPW. The reason that MPW's management is now expecting full repayments relates to the fact that Yale New Haven - after a long court battle with Prospect - reached a settlement. Yale will pay $45 million to Prospect and Yale will be released from buying the three Connecticut hospitals that they agreed on purchasing, all the way back in 2022. But more interesting is the following:

Prospect struck a deal to sell its Connecticut facilities. For those wondering, these three hospitals are the ones I listed above. This means that we are now in the final chapter of the Prospect story. All of the current listed Prospect Medical Holdings assets on the MPW properties list are sold (green) or will be sold very soon (red). Sure, it all depends on court approval, but that seems like a formality at this stage.

I already expected (and wrote about) the high probability that Medical Properties Trust could get its DIP-loans back from Prospect, but it's always nice to see it actually happening.

Regarding the hospitals still up for sale, we recently learned that Hartford HealthCare is willing to pay $86.1 million to acquire Manchester Memorial Hospital and Rockville General. It is important to note that Prospect Medical Holdings paid $105 million for these two facilities, back in 2016. A spokesperson for Hartford HealthCare said the following:

Every hospital that has joined Hartford HealthCare has experienced measurable positive results that benefit patients...

Manchester Memorial Hospital is a 249 bed acute-care community hospital. From what I can find, Rockville General Hospital has 102 beds. This means that the sale price is $245,300 per bed. In a bit, I will show you that at the current valuation of MPW's share price, this is still a pretty positive picture when talking about the value of MPW's portfolio.

Medical Properties Trust is extremely undervalued by the Market

I have written countless articles on Medical Properties Trust. It is almost impossible for me to incorporate all these numbers and calculations in every article. So before continuing with yet another way of showing you how undervalued MPW's stock still is, let me give any new readers a couple of links to specific fundamental analysis parts of my previous articles. Those that are still relevant and are worth (re)reading:

  • MPW's resilient and well-performing Joint Ventures (August 2, 2025 article)
  • MPW's dividend and debt-related risks (August 20, 2025 article)
  • MPW's Q4 2026 + $1 billion in cash rent calculation (September 11, 2025 article)

I will now show you that if we take the recent sale of two hospitals to HonorHealth, we can get to a pretty conservative valuation of Medical Properties Trust's equity value. As I showed you in my previous articles, MPW's stock is undervalued no matter what metric you use. But when we add the following perspective of its value, using the 'sale price per bed', we get the same result: undervaluation by a wide margin.

Let's dive in.

HonorHealth bought two hospitals

Back in September last year, we learned that three of the Arizona hospitals that were previously leased to Steward, went to HonorHealth. A non-profit tenant. Since the press statement above speaks about selling two hospitals in Arizona that were among the re-tenanted hospitals post Steward, we can safely say that we are talking about the hospitals that went to HonorHealth.

I am not sure which two hospitals were purchased by HonorHealth, but the three hospitals currently in the MPW properties list are shown below. I have used the 'Phoenix Metropolitan Area' in the picture below, since this is the area that Medical Properties Trust must have used in their press release as well.

Since capital markets are still not that liquid and REITs that are still working on stabilizing their portfolio and operational performance, it makes sense for MPW to focus on selling assets that can draw on potential buyers. So I want to guesstimate which assets are sold to HonorHealth. If I do not convince you with my take on this matter, please share your thoughts in the comment section below. I will be there, as always.

First of all, let's take a look at the amount of beds per hospital at the three hospitals currently leased to HonorHealth in Phoenix:

  • Temple Medical Center: 74 beds
  • Four Peaks (formerly Mountain Vista): 178 beds
  • Florence Medical Center: 36 beds

So, $50 million was the approx. sale for two of these three hospitals, right? First of all, it makes sense that the renamed hospital is sold. Especially considering the size of the deal. I guess that Temple Medical Center is the second hospital that has been sold. Why? Well, we are talking about a combined 252 beds that got sold at $50 million. We are getting around $198,000 per bed. Medical Properties Trust currently has 39,000 licensed beds that it reports on its front page. Using the average $198,000 per bed, multiplied by 39,000 beds, will give us an estimated value of MPW's portfolio: $12.474 billion. I could use the Florence Medical Center (36 beds) instead of Temple Medical Center (74 beds) because we don't know yet which hospitals were sold. But this gives us $234,000 per bed. In line with the 'per bed sale price' of $245,300 that we got from the latest Prospect sale at the beginning of this article.

I prefer to stick with the lower end of the calculation, so we will assume that Temple Medical Center was sold. Just to be safe.

Let me be clear: we are getting this arbitrary valuation using (bears should agree) pretty pessimistic numbers. First of all, I skipped on the smaller Florence Medical Center so I won't inflate the price per bed. Second, we are talking about hospitals that were previously leased to Steward. Since Steward's management - as did Prospect's management - financially drained their hospitals, these three hospitals could not have been top tier. Just as the freshly "sold" hospitals Hartford HealthCare is about to take over from Prospect. But still, using these distressed assets in the current economic environment, really speaks in favor of the intrinsic value of MPW's portfolio.

To give u an example, just look at the Q1 2025 conference call transcript below, regarding HonorHealth. The three hospitals in the Phoenix Metro Area that we are talking about really needed (a lot of) work done. Meaning that the $50 million sale of two of these hospitals is probably not indicative for the entire portfolio of MPW. The value of the entire portfolio will probably be a lot higher.

I prefer to use a certain margin of safety when we try to assess the value of turbulent (deep-value) REITs like Medical Properties Trust. Let's take the value of MPW's "hard" assets ($11.9 billion). Now we add back the accumulated depreciation and amortization since real estate typically gains value over time and depreciation needs to get added back. We arrive at around $13.5 billion in hard asset value. But since MPW reports Real estate assets - at cost (10-Q, page 34) and reports $13.04 billion, I prefer to go with this number. Simply because it is a lower number and - again - I prefer to create a margin of safety.

Since Steward Health Care was the prime reason that Medical Properties Trust got shorted very heavily and the stock got hammered, the fact that I use former Steward hospitals to calculate the "sale price per hospital bed" must be liked by the MPW bears. We arrived at approximately $12.5 billion. Pretty close to the $13.04 billion in reported Real estate assets - at costs done by management. Especially since we use former Prospect and Steward assets to "guesstimate" the value per Hospital bed (and therefore for the entire 63,000 bed portfolio of MPW).

Now let us add all the other line-items on the balance sheet. I have already shown that we can expect one more impairment of $660 million related to the Prospect bankruptcy. I also subtract the $825 million in Straight-line rent receivable. I am "guesstimating" the value of MPW. If I add the straight-line rent, I would be double-counting, since I already work with the asset-level value of the hospitals (using the sale price per bed). Doing so, we arrive at around $15.635 billion.

When we subtract the Q2 2025 $10.3 billion in debt, we arrive at around $5.335 billion in equity value. As of 10/23/2025 we can see that equity is trading at +/- $3 billion. This leaves 78% in share price appreciation in order for the equity to trade around the "market value" per bed.

Sure, you can take the calculation with a grain of salt. It is a bit arbitrary to just take a sale price of a hospital, divide it by the number of hospital beds and extrapolate this value to the entire portfolio. But still, we get around the same "per bed sale price" using two totally different transactions. And when I make this calculation, we get a pretty reasonable value of the entire portfolio that aligns pretty well with the value of MPW's assets as reported on the balance sheet.

Proof that equity should be trading at least 80% higher

I haven't mentioned this in a previous article, but during the first six months of the year, Medical Properties Trust locked in a 38% gain on real estate by selling three facilities and an ancillary facility. Let me be clear, we are not talking about the difference between the purchase versus sale price. We are talking about the difference between the sale price and the carrying value on the balance sheet. Since depreciation lowers the carrying amount of real estate, a "gain on real estate" can easily be made.

What does this mean and why is it important? Well, even though it is hard to assess the true gain (or loss) on sale based on cash numbers, we can still see - using anecdotical prove from sales done - that the current carrying value of the real estate assets on the balance sheet are at least not overstating the value of the portfolio. In fact, we have data coming from three deals that give me the impression that equity should be trading higher than 80%, from a valuation perspective. I know that sentiment, distrust in management and the high short interest in this stock also do a lot of harm to the share price.

Closing remarks: it's time for shorts to leave the battleground

I applaud all the sophisticated investors that showed the market that Medical Properties Trust, back in 2022, was a true value trap. I really do. The amount of research done by Viceroy Research was a great read, even though they didn't cover any positives regarding Medical Properties Trust. Which makes sense, if you are betting on a falling share price. But smart money probably already moved on from Medical Properties Trust a long time ago. I can't really come up with good arguments for any sophisticated (retail) investor to go short on Medical Properties Trust now. It makes no sense to short a REIT that is in full recovery mode.

Since my last article (where I showed the short interest using the 8/15/2025 numbers), the percentage of float shorted dropped another 2.5 percentage points. I expect that this number to keep dropping going forward.

Good news for the shorts is the fact that the days to cover have been decreasing. Showing that running to the exit, in the event of a short squeeze, will be more manageable.

When you take a look at the graph from SL Green Realty (SLG), you can see that this office REIT also experienced a high amount of short interest back in 2023. I bought the stock at $20 because the stock got punished way too hard. But when SLG proved to the market that it could survive and fundamentals started to improve, shares expanded by 275%. in less than two years.

I don't expect a short squeeze when talking about Medical Properties Trust. These events are very rare and I don't really like to see investors lose a lot of money this way. Instead, I expect that Medical Properties Trust will keep improving its operational performance. We are now post-Steward and post-Prospect. We should see further improvement when the Q3 2025 report gets released. A rapid share price appreciation can happen. I do expect Medical Properties Trust to outperform the REIT sector in the coming quarters. Simply because the stock can capitalize on shorts exiting their positions, the Fed cutting rates (and MPW is still pretty leveraged) and overall rent ramp-up coming from post-Steward tenants as well as all the Prospect hospitals that are sold and will pay rent going forward.

As I have mentioned in a few of my previous articles on MPW, I will sell the bulk of my position when shares hit $7. Due to the fact that management gets a lot of compensation when they get to this price level. Even though I see a way higher intrinsic value per share from a fundamental perspective, I am currently holding a pretty large position in Medical Properties Trust. Management has not been very transparent in recent years and their equity investments in distressed tenants ruined a lot of value. Sure, I gave a lot of arguments that showed that management was also "caught by surprise" as well, but still.

Management knows that whatever metric you use (value per bed, P/FFO multiples or dividend growth models), $7 per share is within reach. And yet, even at that price, the market would still be discounting MPW's intrinsic value by a wide margin.

For patient investors, that disconnect isn't a reason to worry. It's an opportunity worth capitalizing on.

data.title: AST SpaceMobile Stock: Coming Back Down To Earth
data.description:
Thesis Summary

AST SpaceMobile, Inc. (NASDAQ:ASTS) has seen its stock price skyrocket this year, following some strategic deals, commercial execution and increased investor attention.

If ASTS achieves reliable service and scales launches on budget, the revenue opportunity through carrier revenue share could be large.

On the other hand, further dilution and competitive pressure could become an existential threat. Earnings next month will be pivotal for the company.

Ultimately, I think ASTS will succeed, but the stock's valuation may have gotten out of hand.

What does ASTS do?

AST SpaceMobile is building a low-Earth-orbit (LEO) constellation of very large phased-array satellites, also known as BlueBird.

The idea is that these will be able to act like cell towers in space. This means that smartphones can connect directly with AST’s space satellites.

This means that current network carriers could quite seamlessly transition to using ASTS’s satellites.

Why would they do this?

Simple, global coverage without the need to build out expensive infrastructure around the world.

What’s behind the recent rally?

Three catalysts have reignited interest in the company.

Strategic Deals

AT&T’s definitive commercial agreement through 2030 de-risked go-to-market; more recently, Verizon joined to add space-based service using its low-band spectrum, a notable endorsement from another U.S. major.

Visible hardware progress

After the first five BlueBirds launched in September 2024, AST has continued prepping additional satellites (e.g., BlueBird 6 & 7 shipment seen publicly), with guidance from management and trade press suggesting a multi-month launch cadence into 2026.

Proof Of Concept And Scalability

AST has proved that this technology is viable, and it has now been endorsed by large companies. And while it still remains unclear if this is scalable, management has laid out a clear and aggressive growth plan for next year.

Valuation

The question now is whether this stock is worth buying right now after more than doubling in the last year.

This is a nascent industry, technology and company, so it’s not easy to value.

There aren’t many analysts covering this, but those that do seem to believe EPS could turn positive by 2027. Revenue projections also sit at around $3 billion by 2030.

How would this materialize?

Let’s assume 20–60 million paying covered users by 2030 across partners globally.

At what we could estimate as a wholesale price of maybe $2-$4 per user, that gets us close to the $2 billion in revenues at mid-range.

If ASTS executes and grows into $1–2 billion revenue later this decade with improving unit economics, a 10x EV/Sales range is plausible.

That would give us a valuation of around $20 billion, which is already below today’s market cap.

But with revenues closer to $3-4 billion today the price could make sense.

Technical Analysis

From a technical price standpoint, we can see that the stock became very overbought and is now testing the 50 EMA.

This looks like it will be broken as we open this morning, and meaningful support won’t come into play until around $50.

Below that, there’s a lot more support at $20 an area in which the company has consolidated.

I think at $50, and based on our valuation, this is an interesting buying opportunity. At $2,0 I’d be ready to build a more significant position.

Earnings And Outlook

Management and industry reporting have pointed to a plan for ongoing BlueBird launches roughly every 1–2 months on average as production scales, aiming toward a 45–60 satellite constellation through 2026 to enable broader service.

The next earnings, expected on November 11th, will give us a clearer picture of just how fast ASTS can scale.

The other important consideration is financing. While the company has secured around half a billion in cash.

Risks

There are numerous risks with the ASTS/s investment thesis.

Dilution is one, given the capital-intensive nature of the business. On the other hand, there can be notable execution risk. One failed launch or negative headline could send the stock crashing down.

There’s also competition, notably from Starlink, and customer concentration could also become an issue moving forward.

Final Thoughts

All in all, AST is a speculative play right now, which has likely run too far. Market exuberance may have peaked for now, but I have no doubt that this could do very well in the future, and it would be a strong buy at a more reasonable valuation and once the technicals have reset.

data.title: EZBC: Big Outflows As Bitcoin Peaks (Double Downgrade)
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig.

Investment Thesis

Franklin Bitcoin ETF (BATS:EZBC) was founded on January 11, 2024, and it is an exchange-traded fund ("ETF") that tracks the price of Bitcoin (BTC-USD) (net of fees). In essence, it closely follows the successes and failures of Bitcoin and is Franklin Templeton’s play to capture the rapid retail demand for the crypto asset. If the world’s largest cryptocurrency goes up, EZBC tends to go up. When Bitcoin dips, EZBC does as well.

Lately, Bitcoin has been struggling, falling by almost 15% over the last ~10 days. The price of the token peaked at nearly $126,000 on Sunday, October 5, as onlookers became increasingly more excited about “Uptober.” Heading into 2025, Bitcoin had finished in the green in six consecutive Octobers, dating back to 2018, and nine of the last 10 Octobers finished in the positive.

However, not long after reaching the all-time high, the price has dropped sharply, triggering a $530 million outflow across all Bitcoin ETFs. Now, Bitcoin’s price sits right around $110,000.

Given this, I think we have reached the top. Investors are starting to pull funds out of the crypto currency. This could create institutional downward pressure on the Bitcoin market. This is not an environment to be bullish in.

To me, this is a warning sign, not an indicator of resilience. Bitcoin has always gone through four-year boom-bust cycles. I think we are at the top of a boom cycle now.

With this, shares are downgraded from a strong buy to a hold. I believe Bitcoin has peaked.

Why I’m Doing Follow-Up Coverage

When I last wrote on EZBC back in early February, I took a stance indicating that shares of the Bitcoin ETF were a strong buy. Since then, shares have increased by about 13%, but there was about a 20% bump when Bitcoin peaked earlier this month.

As I mentioned, Bitcoin has been trending downward over the past two weeks, which has brought much more attention to ETFs like EZBC. The ETF is a passive fund, meaning that it does not try to time the ebbs and flows of the Bitcoin market. As investors deposit more funds to invest in Bitcoin via the fund, the fund buys more of the token. When investors pull their funds, vice-versa.

According to Bitcoin.com, there were $2.7 billion in net inflows into Bitcoin ETFs during the roughly first 10 days of the month.

EZBC has been flying under the radar, however I think the cryptocurrency has peaked for the cycle. With this, I think this presents us with an opportunity to circle back on EZBC and explain why my stance on the Bitcoin ETF has shifted so drastically since my last piece of coverage on EZBC in February.

Like other Bitcoin ETFs I have written on, I think the gains are largely behind us.

ETFs Begin Selling

On Thursday, October 16th, (as the price of Bitcoin continued to decline) outflows from Bitcoin ETFs surged to $530 million. This effort was led by big name ETFs like (ARKB), (FBTC), (IBIT), and (GBTC). This major outflow event indicated a decline in institutional interest. I think this is a sign that ETF investors are starting to sour on crypto currency.

Really, the strong flows from Bitcoin ETFs have been one of the biggest drivers of upward pressure on the world’s oldest crypto currency. I think this pressure is reversing. The outflows are a key part of that. EZBC is part of this story.

Earlier this month, I believe the bubble popped, with Bitcoin ETFs pulling in $2.7 billion in net inflows as part of what fans deemed “Uptober,” a creative way to project that the price of Bitcoin would go up during October. I think this $2.7 billion came in right at the peak as the bubble popped.

In essence, and as I have written on before with other Bitcoin ETFs, I think there is a mounting pile of evidence that we are at the top for Bitcoin prices.

As someone who was a long-time Bitcoin bull, it's easy to see how this cycle could be "different." In fact, many have called for this cycle to be the first five-year Bitcoin bull run cycle (whereas all previous cycles were four years).

Unfortunately, I don’t think this will be true. Bitcoin now suffers from the risks of quantum hacking before the next potential bull market gets into full swing. As I wrote before:

Quantum hacking is starting to become a huge threat to Bitcoin’s cryptography. Bitcoin relies on the ECDSA (elliptic curve digital signature) algorithm, which conventional computers cannot break (requiring ~2^128 operations). However, a powerful quantum computer running Shor’s algorithm could solve this in polynomial time (exponentially less time than a classical computer). This means they can derive private keys from public keys within minutes. Once a Bitcoin address’ public key is revealed, I believe a quantum owned by an attacker will compute the private key and steal funds. This would be devastating for the cryptocurrency.

On top of this, the risk/reward appeal for Bitcoin is no longer there like it used to be. Like any asset that has become richly valued, buying more at this price just doesn’t make sense to me.

Valuation

Because EZBC is an ETF and not a typical company, my valuation really doesn’t follow the normal pattern that many of my previous articles have followed. ETFs do not have price-to-earnings ratios, PEG ratios and other metrics like normal companies do.

Instead of looking at traditional metrics, we have to look at the ETFs risk and momentum metrics.

Seeking Alpha Quant gives EZBC an overall risk grade of a D-. Of course, this means that (compared to other funds) the ETF has a high level of risk. Now, with Bitcoin likely past what I think is the cycle peak, the risk-reward is no longer favorable. It's why I have downgraded shares to a hold.

EZBC standard deviation grade is an F. EZBC has a standard deviation percentage of 47.83, which is nearly 300% higher than the sector median of just 12.84.

On the other hand, EZBC has a momentum grade of an A, according to Seeking Alpha Quant. The company’s one-month total return and one-month price performance both have an F grade, but the six-month and one-year total return and price performance grades are A- and A+, respectively.

This makes sense because Bitcoin is down over the past month, but is up significantly over the past six months and one year. I expect more of these grades to coverage on an F as the price of Bitcoin decreases (given I think we are at the top of the cycle)

So what do I think the ETF is worth now? To be frank, I really don’t know anymore. If Bitcoin can (long run) answer its quantum computing risk questions, I think there is a five-plus-year bull case to be made once we see the market cool off from this bull run. Until then, I really don’t know what the digital asset is worth anymore.

Bull Thesis

To be clear, I think it's fairly likely at this point that the Bitcoin bull run has reached its peak. With this, any bull thesis relies on Bitcoin rebounding (since EZBC is basically entirely comprised of Bitcoin).

For this to happen, we’d need a catalyst for Bitcoin to rise. The most likely catalyst would be for global interest rates to fall (as the Fed has indicated they will let happen through their subsequent rate cut cycles). This should (on paper) increase global liquidity and, in turn, push more dollars to compete for a finite amount of Bitcoin causing it to rise.

Sounds straightforward right? Yes, there is just one issue. Global liquidity is likely peaking. How is this possible?

Global liquidity is a function of two things: central banks (and governments) pumping excess cash into the economy, plus private individuals and institutions pumping more money/liquidity into the system.

With global central banks indicating they will be cutting interest rates, the first seems plausible. The second? Not as much.

For global liquidity analysis, I look at liquidity newsletters like Michael Howell’s Global Liquidity watch.

In his research, while liquidity is rising now, it looks like liquidity in the U.S. is starting to peak. I think this will cause downward pressure on Bitcoin.

Bottom Line

EZBC’s role as a Bitcoin ETF means the fund is almost entirely dependent on the price of Bitcoin. As the price of the cryptocurrency token peaks, I think this is a sign that the best performance for EZBC (as an investor) is behind us.

I believe that investors should treat this ETF with caution. I’m not yet to the point of a sell, but I certainly am not as bullish as I used to be regarding EZBC.

Really, I think global liquidity is starting to recede, which means Bitcoin (which is closely correlated with EZBC) could see some downward pressure. Already, we’re starting to see this a little (I think) based on the 10% drop since the peak this month.

Bitcoin has been an incredible asset to hold over the last three years since it bottomed in October of 2022. Since 2024 (when EZBC was launched), it's been an incredible investment as well.

Now, I think the returns are behind us. I think it's time to take profits. With this, Franklin Bitcoin ETF shares are downgraded to a hold.

data.title: Bristol-Myers Squibb: This High FCF Yield Is The Best Value In Healthcare
data.description:
Introduction

In my quest to find the best value in the healthcare sector, Bristol-Myers Squibb has been one of the names that came up quite a lot. The company’s stock is currently following the same kind of decline as its peers, to the tune of 23% YTD, while having some of the best valuation metrics. I want to see if it is really a bargain for shareholders or a potential value trap if it can not surmount the future patent cliffs.

Bristol-Myers Squibb (NYSE: NYSE:BMY) operates as a major biopharmaceutical company that has a vast portfolio of blockbuster drugs such as Eliquis and Opdivo. It has become renowned for its deal-making abilities as well as its keen eyes for partnerships that have allowed the company to operate since the 1850s.

Operation Overview

Looking at each therapeutic area, BMY operates in Oncology, Hematology, Immunology, Cardiovascular and Neuroscience, which is certainly a broad array of opportunities in the health market.

Regarding Oncology, it is currently the largest and most prominent target area for BMY, as it pioneered immuno-oncology, a strategy to use the body’s own immune system to fight cancer. Maintaining the company’s leadership in the sector has become critical and the focus has also been towards precision medicine, which is basically developing targeted therapies for specific mutations and that was heavily bolstered by the Mirati Therapeutics acquisition. BMY is also expanding into ADCs, and radiopharmaceuticals as well, and the current main offerings include Opdivo, Yervoy and Krazati.

For Hematology, the area became a key pillar for the company following the massive $74B acquisition of Celgene back in 2019. This has made BMY one of the dominant presences in treatments for Myeloma and further cell therapy needs. The goal is to leverage the multiple Myeloma leadership that BMY currently gains from to expand into next-generation oral therapies. Key products include Revlimid and Pomalyst, which both target multiple myeloma.

On the Immunology side, BMY is currently undergoing a strategic shift focusing on a more specialized pipeline as it will also spin off five assets, such as a late-stage drug for lupus, into a new independent company, in partnership with Bain Capital. Key products include Orencia, used to treat arthritis and sclerosis, respectively.

Cardiovascular has also been recently revitalized with a focus on precision medicine for heart conditions as it capitalizes on its blockbuster anticoagulant, Eliquis. Furthermore, the precision side has been launched by the $13.1B acquisition of MyoKardia. And for Neuroscience, BMY also made the $14B acquisition of Karuna Therapeutics to be the centerpiece of this segment as it is making massive investments to build a leading position. Mainly, it is pursuing treatment for conditions such as Alzheimer’s disease.

Current Dynamics

Bristol-Myers Squibb is currently facing a typical pharma dilemma as its Growth Portfolio is trying to outpace the steep decline of its Legacy Portfolio, which has faced patent expirations and massive competition from generics. As to have a clearer view, the patent losses are on drugs representing nearly half of 2024 sales that include Revlimid and Polamyst, which should decline starting in 2026. Eliquis will also see patent losses down the line in 2028. And as you could have seen in the previous segment of my analysis, BMY has done a lot of deals to try to revitalize the company, and I believe that the stock is currently down as investors don’t want to take the risk of waiting for its investments to pay out and replace the loss of revenue, and that’s where our opportunity lies.

Though I’ll say that based on the company’s deal track record, BMY has been highly accretive and capable of building strong pipelines that should offset the future loss of income. For example, the 2019 Celgene acquisition pushed BMY further into blood-related diseases, thus allowing the company to expand into niche health issues that allow for strong pricing power, offering an advantage for the company when drug prices are particularly in the spotlight. BMY also made an intelligent move to have a fairly large portion of its drugs derived from biologic production, which creates a large hurdle for generic production as it requires a higher level of production costs to effectively market them.

A high risk could also come from Opdivo, which represented close to a fifth of the company’s sales and is seeing massive headwinds ahead with patent expiry in the EU in 2026 and the US in 2028, while also having Medicare pricing negotiations in 2029. So, how is BMY proactively trying to fill the hole? First, it is trying to extend the franchise, with a newer subcutaneous version and shifting a projected 40% of its US patients to this new formulation before the patent expiry, protecting a large portion of revenue into the 2030s. Furthermore, BMY is expanding its cancer offering with a novel cancer immunotherapy with BioNTech that is currently in phase 3. All of these measures should largely offset the loss in revenue for Opdivo.

BMY is also set to release its earnings by the end of the month, and we should continue to monitor the current dynamics for the legacy portfolio and see if it can keep its margins despite generic erosion. I will certainly look to see how Eliquis and Revlimid are faring, while on the growth side, I want to see if Opdivo is on track to reach $10B in sales before patent expiry.

Valuation

BMY makes a real case of a massive undervaluation, even more than Pfizer, which I extensively covered as the pharma company to buy regarding the undervaluation case. It currently has a bottom-of-the-market P/E Non-GAAP of 6.48, while its EV/EBITDA is also at the lowest end at 6.59. Furthermore, BMY is a great FCF generator, drastically increasing its FCF/share from 77 cents in 2015 to now reaching $7.18, translating in a 16.46% FCF yield, one of the highest levels available in the pharma industry. Furthermore, just like Pfizer (PFE), BMY is currently offering a generous dividend yield for patient investors at 5.68%. And this dividend has grown relatively well at a rate of 5.3% CAGR over the past 10 years.

Growth-wise, BMY has seen its overall sales stall since the Celgene deal, with total revenue hovering between $46.4B and $48.3B since 2021. And this year seems to be no different, with BMY raising its revenue guidance to reach between $46.5B and $47.5B for the FY2025. Now that is mainly due to the Legacy portfolio facing such a sharp decline in the immediate term, while the Growth portfolio picks up steam with growth currently seeing up to 18% Y/Y rates. Now this means that BMY is highly dependent on M&A and pipeline successes to justify this growth story ahead, which makes it riskier on paper for potential investors. Though I’ll say that BMY has one of the best track records in terms of dealmaking, with the most recent ones, such as Karuna are yet to pay off and provide great FCF to the company. Furthermore, BMY’s pipeline of next-gen drugs is one of the best in class and has the best chances to offset patent losses.

And again, profitability-wise, BMY is able to shine in a crowded pharma sector with its gross margin being the second best among its peers at 73.92%, slightly lower than AstraZeneca (AZN). Though it has decreased since 2021, this is mainly due to the shift in product mix as well as the loss of market exclusivity for Revlimid. The focus on the growth side means that at this particular moment, BMY will see lower gross margins over the near future, though it still remains best in class. The current strength of the legacy portfolio also means that despite seeing patent cliffs, at this particular juncture, it is able to command one of the highest FCF margins at 30.6%. This also translates in relatively strong capital efficiency metrics with BMY posting a ROE of 29.32% and a ROCE of 22.9%.

There is also the debt situation to assess, as it is the toll to have when a company does a fair amount of deals. Net debt has risen drastically since the Celgene deal, now reaching $37B. Though the high level of profitability from the firm allows it to have a relatively safe measure of debt with a current Net Debt/EBITDA below 2, and the strong levels of FCF are telling me that the company will easily manage its debt level.

Risks

The biggest risks in the pharma industry always come from patent expiration and the ensured generic threats that will drastically lower the pricing power that these companies can have from patent protection. If the current growth set of drugs does not pan out as expected and does not replace the legacy portfolio of BMY, we could see the profitability levels drastically decline.

There is also the question of pricing control in the US, which has become a major issue that the current Trump administration wants to tackle. It is not yet set in stone how the administration wants to proceed, but, for example, Eliquis was one of the first drugs selected for Medicare price negotiations under the Inflation Reduction Act.

Conclusion

I believe that the current valuation behind BMY makes the risks worth taking. BMY is facing critical patent cliffs but is proactively taking steps to mitigate this and drive growth long-term. It is also paying a generous yield for patient investors, waiting to ride the rebound. I rate Bristol-Myers Squibb as a Strong Buy.

data.title: BNY Mellon Appreciation Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
“As markets may face volatility with geopolitical, monetary, and tariff policy outcomes unfolding, we remain confident in the long-term growth trajectories and fundamental business strength across our portfolio.”

Market Review

The S&P 500 Index gained 8.12% in the third quarter of 2025 as investors were encouraged by trade deal announcements, a strong earnings season that conveyed resilient growth despite tariff costs, and the shift by the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) towards monetary easing.

The third quarter witnessed a litany of trade deals announced between the U.S. and its trade partners; these deals de-escalated tensions and further buoyed global equity markets. Equity markets reacted positively after an agreement between the U.S. and Japan was struck that included a 15% tariff on Japanese imports (versus the 25% initially announced) and reduced tariffs on U.S. exports. In addition, 15% tariffs on most imported goods from the European Union and South Korea were also announced, while India saw a 50% punitive tariff imposed in response to the country’s ongoing purchase of Russian oil despite sanctions. In the meantime, trade negotiations continued with China over sensitive issues, including the sale of graphics processing units and access to China’s rare earth minerals.

The second quarter earnings season revealed a continuation of two themes: first, a resilient economy despite macroeconomic headwinds; and second, continued investments in artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure. Broadly, corporate sales and earnings came in above consensus forecasts, beating expectations. Results from the financials sector indicated the consumer was still in good shape, with virtually no credit deterioration. Bank results were strong, anchored by increased trading and transaction activity. Tariff impacts were seen across the consumer staples and discretionary sectors, with companies that import products and materials from overseas implementing small price increases to cover a portion of the increased costs and consumers continuing to trade down in response to higher prices. In the health care sector, higher care costs weighed on managed care providers. Within industrials, enacted tariffs weighed on automakers with global supply chains while companies that have exposure to the AI datacenter buildout benefited. Large technology companies reported benefits from deploying AI internally and for clients, which boosted revenue growth. These companies also expanded investment plans for additional AI resources to meet growing demand, as consumers and enterprises rapidly adopt AI. Overall, companies reported earnings growth while dealing with a slowing consumer and a dynamic tariff environment. Investors took solace that tariffs did not materially hurt consumer demand or negatively affect profit margins.

Disappointing jobs data in the U.S., including a downward revision of 911,000 jobs created in the past year, revealed an unexpected weakness in the labor market. The Fed acknowledged the deteriorating labor market and voted to cut the Federal Funds rate by 25 basis points (bps) to a 4.00-4.25% range in its September meeting. This marked the first rate cut by the Fed since December 2024, and the agency also telegraphed the potential for two additional 25 bps rate cuts through the end of 2025. The Fed has a dual mandate to keep inflation in its target range and support the domestic job market, and the recent weakening of the U.S. labor market has shifted the Fed’s focus to sustaining maximum employment levels after years of being solely focused on taming inflation.

While a myriad of risks remain, including the ultimate outcome of trade deals and ongoing geopolitical tensions, investors welcomed the trade, earnings, and monetary policy developments made during the quarter.

data.title: Old Republic: Steady Results At A Fair Valuation
data.description:
Shares of Old Republic International (NYSE:ORI) have been a solid performer over the past year, rising about 13%. However, shares fell over 3% in early trading Thursday as investors digested its Q3 earnings release. While the company reported solid results, these results are unlikely to calm fears that there could be peak margins in some business lines. With updated financials, now is a good time to see if this is a pullback that should be bought.

In the company’s third quarter, Old Republic earned $0.78 per share, up 10% from last year, as revenue grew by 8% to $2.3 billion. Results were also better than the $0.76 that analysts were expecting. Old Republic is primarily a property & casualty insurer focused on commercial markets, and it also has a title insurance division. As you can see below, its specialty P&C business is the primary source of earnings.

Looking first at underwriting results, the company reported a combined ratio of 95.3%, about 30bps worse than last year. As a reminder, a combined ratio of 100% means an insurer is breaking even on its underwriting operation. In this case, ORI is making about a 5% margin on its underwriting. This is at the high end of the company’s target, and the modest increase in the combined ratio is consistent with some rising pricing pressure that is beginning to appear across the industry.

The company had favorable prior-year developments of 2.5%, 1.2% better than last year. Essentially, historic policies have proven to be more profitable than initially modeled, allowing the company to release reserves. I view favorable reserve releases as a sign of conservative underwriting modeling and a key positive. Old Republic has consistently been able to release reserves, maintaining its track record of solid underwriting.

That said, with a larger prior-year benefit, the fact that ORI’s combined ratio is still worse than last year means that current-year performance has gotten noticeably weaker. ORI saw its expense ratio increase by 2.5% to 31.3%, while actual losses were flat at 66.9%. Essentially, policies are performing as well as in prior years, but the cost to write policies (namely, employee compensation) has increased, given wage growth and increased commissions. I would expect this cost to normalize, and I generally am less concerned by increases in underwriting costs and current-year losses.

Looking at segment results, specialty insurance earnings rose by 5% from last year. Its combined ratio here was 94.8%, up from 94%. This division continues to enjoy 90+% retention. It structures its policies so that its commercial clients actually share in some of the underwriting risk by retaining some loss exposure or maintaining exposure in a captive entity. This helps to maintain retention and align interests. Premiums grew to $1.3 billion, up 8% from last year, though underwriting income declined by 4% to $69 million. As you can see below, auto is its primary exposure.

This has been a source of concern because we are beginning to see signs of pricing moderation in this sector. Auto-related premiums surged in 2022-2023 as insurers sought to reclaim lost margin, but they have largely recouped margins. As a result, capital is returning to the segment, which is likely to pressure margins to an extent, especially if tariffs raise vehicle prices (which we are not seeing yet). I generally expect Old Republic to report stable to slightly higher combined ratios over the next year as a result.

In its other business line, title insurance earnings grew by 14%, and its combined ratio fell by 30bps to 96.4%. Premiums grew by 8% to $767 million. ORI is the No. 3 player in this space, and commercial is 22% of title revenue. As you can see below, title revenue boomed post-COVID when rates plunged, leading to a significant rise in homebuying and refinance activity. Since then, it has bounced along the bottom.

Going forward, I expect revenue to remain muted, but there are likely to be some tailwinds. Its commercial focus should help, as we are seeing capital markets activity loosen up in CRE, which may enable an increase in transactions. Plus, mortgage rates are now at the low end of their two-year range. Given difficult affordability metrics, I do not expect this to lead to more homebuying, but at 6%, refinance activity should pick up considerably, which will lead to more title insurance demand. I expect double-digit growth in Q4 and likely into H1 2026 as a result.

Elsewhere, net investment income was $183 million, up by 7%. It has a $16.7 billion investment portfolio. It has about 84% invested in high-quality fixed income. The portfolio has a 99% allocation to investment-grade securities, and ORI has no exposure to real estate or private equity. With a 3.9-year duration, its 4.7% book yield is fairly locked-in even as the Fed cuts rates, and I expect yields to be broadly stable over the next year. Aside from this, it has 16% allocated to equities to generate capital appreciation. Essentially, its fixed-income portfolio covers needs for potential claims to avoid being a forced seller of stocks, allowing it to be a long-term holder of equities. This model has worked well, and ORI has benefited from rising rates, with investment income growing considerably over time.

Old Republic maintains an excellent balance sheet. Debt to capital is just around 20%, a very healthy level. It has raised its dividend 44 straight years, and shares offer a 2.8% dividend yield. During the quarter, it bought back $44 million of stock. I expect limited buyback going forward, given its M&A activity.

Alongside results, Old Republic announced it would be acquiring Everett Cash Mutual after the firm converts out of its mutual status. ECM’s business is focused on farm owners and other agricultural businesses. The mutual has a $126 million book value and about $240 million in premiums, so this is a bolt-on deal, and financial terms were not disclosed. ORI did, however, state the deal will be accretive to both book value and EPS. I expect that ORI is paying no more than $175 million.

Shares currently have a ~$26 book value, so shares are trading at just over 1.5x book value. With modest underwriting pressure from commercial auto but growth in title insurance, I see ORI earning about $3.45-$3.65 next year, assuming no more than 4 more Fed rate cuts. That leaves shares just over 11x earnings. For a company with a safe balance sheet and strong dividend track record, this is not expensive. However, investors can buy Chubb (CB) at a similar valuation, and I view CB as a stronger underwriter, but the sector as a whole is trading at fairly low multiples.

As such, I view ORI as a “Hold.” With a ~9% earnings yield, ORI should be able to deliver at least market-like returns with ongoing dividend increases. That said, for investors seeking capital appreciation, I see stronger companies at similar valuations. If we saw a pullback to $38, I would consider being a buyer, but for now, there is neither a rush to buy nor sell.

data.title: VanEck Mid-October 2025 Bitcoin ChainCheck
data.description:
Bitcoin’s October pullback reflects a liquidity-driven mid-cycle reset. Leverage has normalized, on-chain activity is rising, and digital assets’ macro role continues to strengthen.

Please note that VanEck has exposure to bitcoin.

Three key takeaways for mid-September – mid-October:

  1. Liquidity Drives the Cycle: Global M2 growth continues to explain more than half of Bitcoin’s price variance, reaffirming Bitcoin’s role as an anti-money printing asset. Asian trading hours leading price discovery over the past year suggests that tightening regional liquidity is driving near-term volatility.
  2. Leverage Flush Creates Opportunity: Futures open interest peaked at $52B before cascading liquidations drove Bitcoin’s ~18% drawdown in early October. With leverage now normalized to the 61st percentile and prices near one-year lows relative to gold, we view this as a mid-cycle correction, not the start of a bear market.
  3. Onchain Activity Reflects a Maturing Market: Strong revenue-to-price correlations among L1s and sustained Bitcoin treasury accumulation point to Bitcoin’s maturation, underscoring the asset class’s growing importance in model portfolios.

Chart of the Month

Total Bitcoin Miner Debt (USD Billions)

Source: VanEck estimates, FactSet as of 10/15/2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities named herein.

Bitcoin miners’ total debt has surged over the past 12 months from ~$2.1B in Q2 2024 to ~$12.7B in Q2 2025. Bitcoin mining is a challenging business; without continued investment in the latest ASICs, a miner’s share of the global hash rate deteriorates, resulting in a reduced pro rata share of the finite number of Bitcoin awarded daily. We refer to this dynamic as “the melting ice cube problem”. Historically, miners relied on equity markets, not debt, to fund these steep Capex costs. This stems from the fact that miners’ revenues are difficult to underwrite as they rely almost entirely on the price of Bitcoin, which is speculative. Importantly, equity tends to be a more expensive form of capital than debt.

As we have covered extensively since Q3 2024, miners have progressively pivoted greater amounts of power from mining Bitcoin to supporting the energy-hungry AI/HPC data center business. In doing so, miners have secured more predictable cash flows backed by multi-year contracts. The relative predictability of these cash flows has enabled miners to tap into debt markets, diversifying their revenues from Bitcoin’s speculative and cyclical prices and lowering their overall cost of capital.

What does this mean for Bitcoin’s network? Rather than being a threat to network hash rate, we think AI’s priority for electrons is a net benefit to Bitcoin. Bitcoin mining remains an easy way to quickly monetize excess electricity in remote or developing energy markets, effectively subsidizing the development of data centers that are designed with AI/HPC convertibility in mind. In addition, AI inference experiences cyclical demand over the course of the day based on human activity. A number of Bitcoin miners we have spoken to have reported that they are exploring ways to monetize excess electrical capacity when demand for AI inference is low. In doing so, for certain use cases, they may be able to offset or even eliminate costly components of backup electrical power systems such as diesel generators, which can cost as much as $4M/MW despite running only <1% of the year, effectively internally capturing the value of curtailment that has proven successful in markets like ERCOT. While this remains conceptual, we think it represents a logical next step in the unique synergies between Bitcoin and AI that lead to greater efficiency in the use of capital, both financial and electrical.

Bitcoin Price & Futures Annualized Basis: Bitcoin’s price made new all-time highs above $125K on October 6th before dropping to a low around $105K by the 10th, with its 30-day moving average up 2% on the month. The steep selloff was driven by a mix of macro risks such as developing U.S.-China trade tensions, high levels of futures open interest, and whale profit-taking, triggering a cascade of liquidations (margin calls) in perpetual futures markets. Futures borrowing rates averaged 9% over the last 30 days, 18% higher than the previous month, accompanied by highs in open interest prior to the crash (discussed in detail below). However, without a clear or ongoing “black swan” event in play, this looks more like a mid-cycle selloff than the start of a bear market.

Global Power Consumption & Mining Difficulty: Bitcoin’s global power consumption (+7%) and mining difficulty (+10%) reached new all-time highs this month, reflecting ongoing mining fleet expansions and ASIC upgrades. Though AI-geared GPUs continue to displace Bitcoin mining ASICs located in key AI data center geographies, miners continue to gravitate towards low-cost power.

Transfer Volume from Miners to Exchanges: Transfer volumes from miners to exchanges increased 14% this month versus only 2% in BTC price gains, reflecting miners monetizing their production instead of holding. We think this reflects the steep capex requirements faced by the increasing number of miner AI-pivots as well as de-risking amid price volatility.

Total Crypto Equities’ Market Cap: The MVIS® Global Digital Assets Equity Index (MVDAPP)’s 30-day moving average rose 18% over the past month as miners such as APLD, IREN, and CIFR continued to be rewarded for pivoting scarce power assets toward meeting AI’s growing energy demands.

What Drives Bitcoin’s Price: 3 Key Factors

Allocating to Bitcoin, like most emerging asset classes, remains part art and part science. Yet three measurable factors have consistently explained much of Bitcoin’s price behavior over time: global liquidity, leverage, and onchain activity. Together, these forces provide investors with a practical framework for sizing and timing exposure to digital assets.

1. Global Liquidity

BTC Price Correlates Highly with Global M2

Source: Artemis, FRED as of 10/10/2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities named herein.

Bitcoin’s price has long been tethered to the ebb and flow of global money supply. Since 2014, Bitcoin has exhibited roughly a 0.5 correlation (r² = 0.25) with total global M2 growth, meaning that changes in the availability of fiat currency liquidity have explained a meaningful portion of its long-term returns.

While this relationship tends to weaken during short-lived shocks, such as COVID in 2020, the 2024 election, or the “Tariff Tantrum” of 2025, broader trends in monetary expansion continue to dominate Bitcoin’s cycles. A multivariable regression of the top five fiat currency supplies against Bitcoin price shows that changes in M2 explain more than half (r² = 0.54) of Bitcoin’s variance over the past decade.

Since 2013, global liquidity across the top five currencies has roughly doubled from $50 trillion to nearly $100 trillion, during which Bitcoin’s price has increased over 700x. Among individual currencies, the euro M2 money supply remains the strongest explanatory variable (r = 0.69, t = 10), highlighting Bitcoin’s growing role as a neutral reserve asset amid synchronized currency debasement.

VanEck’s historical macro views align with this data. In March 2023, CEO Jan Van Eck said on CNBC that both gold and Bitcoin looked primed to enter a multi-year bull cycle as the Fed neared the end of tightening. In that framework, the firm’s thesis was that Bitcoin functions as “digital gold,” poised to benefit when banks showed weakness as liquidity was setting up to expand again amid looming rate cuts.

When it comes to Bitcoin price discovery, regional market dynamics have shifted meaningfully over the past two years. As shown below, Asian trading hours now lead global BTC returns after lagging Western sessions earlier this cycle and in the 2020-2022 cycle. This year, we observed that Asia led the recent move higher through late summer and is now also leading the latest decline. This rotation may reflect tightening liquidity in Asian markets, as central banks in India and China defend their currencies at the expense of domestic money growth. This pattern is consistent with Bitcoin’s role as an ‘anti-money printing’ asset within global liquidity cycles.

Annualized Average Hourly Returns of BTC By Trading Session

Source: Glassnode as of 10/10/2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities named herein.

2. Leverage

BTC Price Correlates Strongly to BTC Futures Open Interest (OI)

Source: Artemis as of 10/10/2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities named herein.

Leverage is both a driver and a consequence of Bitcoin’s monetization process. Since October 2020, nearly 73% of Bitcoin’s price variance has been explained by changes in futures open interest (t = 71), underscoring the reflexive relationship between speculative positioning and spot price. Periods of exuberant leverage have historically preceded corrections, while orderly deleveraging phases have marked attractive entry points.

BTC Futures Open Interest (OI) Climbed 2.5x YoY Before Crashing in October

Source: Glassnode as of 10/16/2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities named herein.

As of early October 2025, futures leverage sat near its 95th percentile, with the cash collateral backing Bitcoin futures at record highs (~$145B). Open interest peaked at $52B on October 6th before falling to $39B on October 10th following an 8-hour, 20% BTC drawdown, a reminder of how margin calls can cascade through the system. Notably, leverage has never sustained levels above 30% for more than 75 days, suggesting a limit on sustained risk appetite. As of mid-October, Bitcoin’s futures leverage ratio is at the 61st percentile of its historical ranges over the past 5.25 years.

At the same time, the composition of leverage has matured. Greater participation from institutions, miners, and ETF market makers has shifted activity toward regulated venues like CME, where longer-dated and hedging-oriented contracts dominate. Leverage remains a dual-edged sword, amplifying drawdowns but also reflecting growing confidence in Bitcoin as a financial asset.

Against the backdrop of this month’s deleveraging event and Bitcoin prices reaching lows relative to Gold not seen since October 2024, we view the current market as a buying opportunity.

3. Onchain Activity

1 Year Correlation Blockchain Revenue with Token Price

Source: Artemis as of 10/10/2025. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities named herein.

While macro liquidity and market structure provide indicators of Bitcoin’s shorter-term cycles, which generally drive risk appetite across the broader crypto market, onchain fundamentals offer the clearest window into crypto’s real adoption. Across major networks, token prices have shown statistically significant relationships with the revenues their blockchains generate, evidence that usage and value are intertwined.

Among large chains, Solana exhibits the strongest link between network revenue and token performance (r2 = 0.71, t = 30), while Binance Chain’s BNB shows the weakest (r2 = 0.13). The causal direction is complex: higher token prices catalyze more revenue-generating user activity, yet consistent fee generation reinforces long-term valuations. As chains’ app ecosystems mature, they offer new channels for activity, which can improve the relationship between price and onchain revenues. As the newest entrant on the six blockchains in the graph above, Sui’s network developments in DeFi and consumer gaming/NFT apps over the past two years most clearly demonstrate this relationship.

For Bitcoin, onchain metrics like transaction volume and network fees remain less predictive of daily price moves than liquidity or leverage. However, they still serve as tangible proof of network health. We think Bitcoin—and to a lesser extent, Ethereum—are relatively disconnected from these fundamentals versus other blockchain networks due to their growing adoption as store-of-value monetary assets held by off chain treasuries and ETPs. Notwithstanding these factors advantaging BTC and ETH, sustained growth in onchain revenues and user activity provides the most concrete evidence of a blockchain network’s value proposition extending beyond speculation.

Onchain metrics aside, while Bitcoin remains, as Jan van Eck described in 2023, “an eight-year-old child” in its adoption curve, its growing correlation with global liquidity and leverage suggests it is progressing from speculative asset to a macro hedge against fiat currency debasement. Today, Bitcoin is more akin to a teenager; though Bitcoin is growing up, we expect the mood swings to continue in the years ahead.

Practical Takeaways for Investors

With Bitcoin comprising ~2% of global money supply, we believe digital assets can play an increasingly important role in investment portfolios; arguably, owning less than ~2% Bitcoin or other digital assets is implicitly expressing a short position on the asset class. With fiat debasement accelerating in recent years, that is not a bet VanEck is willing to take.

For these reasons, some of our Model Portfolios include exposure to digital assets. We advise systematic allocations within certain risk budgets that have portfolio manager discretion. Current Fact Sheets show Digital Assets allocations at 1.47%, 4.56%, and 6.08% across model portfolios.

In our active strategies, we take a more targeted approach by searching for particular best ideas and buying max fear events. For example, our 3-factor view of Bitcoin helps inform our allocations to Bitcoin and other digital asset ETPs, as well as high-beta sectors like Bitcoin miners. These Bitcoin-informed investment decisions can be combined with allocations to crypto-adjacent sectors like data center, energy, and software companies in an effort to maximize Bitcoin cycles while providing defensive, lower-volatility differentiation.

Disclosures

Definitions

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) is a decentralized digital currency without a central bank or single administrator. It can be sent from user to user on the peer-to-peer Bitcoin network without intermediaries.

Ethereum (ETH-USD) A decentralized platform that enables smart contracts and decentralized applications (dApps) using its native token, Ether.

Solana (SOL-USD) A high-performance blockchain using Proof-of-History and Proof-of-Stake to support fast, low-cost dApps and decentralized finance. BNB (BNB-USD) The native token of the BNB Chain ecosystem (formerly Binance Smart Chain), used for gas fees, staking, and DeFi applications.

Sui (SUI-USD) A high-throughput, low-latency Layer 1 blockchain designed for parallel transaction execution and on-chain asset management.

Avalanche (AVAX-USD) A highly scalable smart contract platform designed for speed and low fees, supporting custom subnets and DeFi applications.

S&P 500 Index is a stock market index of 500 of the largest companies listed on stock exchanges in the United States.

MVDAPP MVIS® Global Digital Assets Equity Index (MVDAPP) tracks the performance of the largest and most liquid companies in the digital assets industry. This is a modified market cap-weighted index, and only includes companies that generate at least 50% of their revenue from digital asset services and products, such as exchanges, payment gateways, mining operations, software services, equipment and technology, digital asset infrastructure, or the facilitation of commerce with the use of digital assets.

Risk Considerations

The value of digital assets, including but not limited to Bitcoin, Ethereum, and other cryptocurrencies, is highly volatile and you can lose your entire principal investment.
