data.title: UTG's Rare Discount: My Favorite High-Yield AI Play Is Too Cheap To Ignore
data.description:
I last wrote about the Reeves Utility Income Fund (NYSE:UTG) back in early August, highlighting it as my favorite AI infrastructure fund. Those reasons included its attractive, consistent, and growing distribution that was hiked earlier this year by an impressive 5.3%, its long-term total return outperformance of the broader utility sector (XLU) and the Morningstar U.S. CEF Equity Index, its prudent use of leverage, its relatively modest management fee for an actively managed fund with an impressive track record, its reasonable valuation that was only at a slight premium to net asset value, and the fact that it was heavily invested in AI infrastructure plays like Vistra Corporation (VST), CenterPoint Energy (CNP), and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) as some of its largest holdings.

However, recently, UTG has sold off aggressively. With a stunning decline of nearly 10% over the past eight days, it now trades at a rare, meaningful discount to NAV. With its discount to NAV reaching a 52-week low, in my view, this makes it a highly compelling buy, and in this article, I will detail why.

Billionaires Are Buying AI Infrastructure—And UTG Is Positioned To Benefit

As I have written about previously in my coverage of UTG, leading billionaire investors, including Brookfield’s (BN)(BAM) Bruce Flatt and BlackRock’s (BLK) Larry Fink, are pouring billions of dollars into AI infrastructure. Not only that, but the leading large-cap tech stocks like NVIDIA (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), and Alphabet (GOOG) are also pouring hundreds of billions of dollars into AI-related CapEx. Overall, this is leading an AI CapEx boom that is having an enormous impact on the broader U.S. economy. For example, JPMorgan Asset Management (JPM) estimated that in the first half of 2025, AI-related CapEx added about 1.1% to U.S. GDP growth, or about 110 basis points. In particular, AI-related hardware investments were up by about 41% year-over-year, and data center construction hit a record annual run rate of $40 billion. Meanwhile, Reuters estimated that CapEx from AI delivered more than one-third of Q2 2025 U.S. GDP growth, with much of that pouring into AI-related infrastructure.

This, of course, is a huge boon to UTG given that at least 80% of its total assets at any time are invested in companies within the utility industry, and beyond that, it has other AI-adjacent businesses. As pointed out last time, it has significant investments in VST and CNP, as well as Constellation Energy (CEG), which make up three of its four largest positions and about 18% of the company’s overall portfolio. VST and CEG, in particular, are leading AI power generators that stand to benefit immensely from the AI boom. Utilities companies in general also stand to benefit, given that they are power generators that will likely benefit from a surge in electricity demand from AI infrastructure.

How UTG Leverages AI And Infrastructure

In addition, UTG’s mandate gives it the flexibility to also invest in real estate-related companies, including data centers that could give it additional ways to leverage the AI boom. In fact, its investment in Deutsche Telekom (OTCQX:DTEGY) as its fifth-largest position, which makes up more than 5% of its portfolio, is another way that it can benefit from the AI boom through edge data centers and fiber, as well as backhaul services.

While UTG is leaning into the AI boom and paying out very attractive and growing distributions that currently give it a 6.43% dividend yield with monthly payouts and a prudent 16.92% effective leverage ratio, UTG has recently pulled back by a stunning 10% over the past eight days.

As such, its discount to net asset value has expanded from trading virtually in line with its NAV to a 52-week low of a -4.48% discount to NAV. For context, its 52-week average is -0.16%, and as the chart below demonstrates, it generally trades in a very tight band with NAV and has even traded at a 4.4% premium at one point over the past 52 weeks. This signals that now is one of the most attractive risk-adjusted times to buy UTG, especially when combining the discounted valuation with the strong macro tailwinds for the business.

Long-Term Outperformance With Smart Leverage

Not only that, but as previously pointed out, the management team has done an exceptional job over time by keeping leverage in a sweet spot where it is high enough to juice the yield and total returns, but not enough to put the fund at significant risk of permanent impairment through margin calls during market downturns.

Over the long term, despite the recent dip to a discount to NAV, it has still outperformed the Morningstar U.S. CEF Equity Index over the past three months, six months, one year, three years, five years, and ten years on a NAV basis. Additionally, it continues to outperform the broader utility space over the long term.

Risks To Watch And Investor Takeaway

Of course, the sharp pullback does show one of the big risks to UTG, namely that it is a CEF, so it can trade in a volatile range relative to its underlying NAV. Even if management does a good job of managing the portfolio and picking stocks in the infrastructure space, the market could still punish it disproportionately and leave shareholders with underperformance. However, as Benjamin Graham once said,

In the short-run, the stock market is a voting machine. Yet, in the long-run, it is a weighing machine.

We believe the same is the case with UTG. It has proven through all sorts of environments that it can deliver sustainable, growing, and attractive dividends while also generating attractive total returns over time.

The other big risk facing UTG is that if the AI CapEx boom goes bust—which there are growing concerns that this could be the case—it may also suffer disproportionately due to the fact that it does employ some leverage and is so heavily concentrated in high-flying players like VST and CEG that are heavily leveraged to the AI CapEx boom. Moreover, the fact that it is a CEF means that it may become significantly out of favor and trade at a steep discount to NAV if these dynamics were to play out.

However, as part of a well-diversified, income-focused portfolio, UTG can be a useful diversification instrument to give income investors leveraged access to the AI boom that they might otherwise lack. As a result, I think UTG could be a good fit for an income-focused, total-return conscious portfolio, and I rate it a Buy right now. As an aside, while we do not own UTG at the moment, if its discount widens further, it could end up being a good fit for our portfolios. In the meantime, we are investing in other AI infrastructure opportunities that offer even better yields and total return risk-reward profiles in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: GSI Technology: Is The APU A Game Changer Or Just Noise In The Market?
data.description:
GSI Technology (NASDAQ:GSIT) issued a major update to its associative processing unit [APU] technology in development on October 20, 2025, sending the share price up 260%+ to over $18/share before settling down to roughly $13/share at the end of the trading day on October 20, 2025, creating significant interest amongst shareholders. Shares have since declined by nearly -40% from the peak share price after GSIT announced on the following day a $50mm share and pre-funded warrant registration, pricing the shares and warrants at $10/share and $9.99/warrant. Given the significant run-up in the share price, I am recommending GSIT shares with a Sell rating, pricing shares at the midcycle premium of $4.33/share at 4.05x eFY27 price/sales. It should be noted that my rating downgrade is purely tied to valuation and not GSIT's current operational state.

GSIT will announce q2’26 earnings October 30, 2025.

You can review my previous coverage of GSI Technology here:

GSI Technology Is Pushing For A Recovery In A Challenging Market

GSI Technology Operational Update

There will be key factors that I will be looking for in eq2’26 earnings:

  • APU CIM applications and potential adoption plan.
  • Go-to-market strategy.
  • Capacity updates to service the addressable market.
  • Technology partnerships for implementation.

The market-moving announcement on October 20, 2025, was the published results of a research paper through Cornell University, finding that GSIT’s APU compute-in-memory architecture can “match GPU-level performance for large-scale AI applications” with 98% lower energy consumption. The press release indicates that the APU performance is comparable to the Nvidia (NVDA) RTX A6000, a GPU designed for desktop workstations.

Taking into consideration the verbiage of the press release and the compared technology, I believe investors should be aware of the full context of the performance comparison. The press release suggested that the APU CIM technology “has the potential to disrupt the $100b AI inference market.” Investors should consider that the study compared GSIT’s APU CIM to Nvidia’s workstation GPU rather than its data center GPUs like the H100, GB200 or GB300, a potentially narrower growth market relative to the AI data center market. For Nvidia, the Professional Visualization market segment was only a $2.1b business in the last twelve months, accounting for only 1.28% of net revenue.

The reason I bring this up isn’t hinting that GSIT may take over market share from Nvidia but rather, raising the question as to why the share prices of major edge compute chipmakers like Intel (INTC), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), or Nvidia didn’t react to the breaking news. Granted, the market for GSIT’s APU may be beyond the workstation, targeting markets like robotics, drones, IoT devices, and aerospace & defense applications that require high-capacity computing while infrastructure resources may be limited. This can particularly be an appealing technology for drones and aerospace & defense technologies, particularly as more equipment becomes electrified.

Drones are quickly becoming a larger interest for national defense and military operations as the battlefield shifts more towards advanced technologies. The Department of War and NASA are currently collaborating on developing autonomous, long-distance cargo transportation drones that can fly beyond visual line of sight.

In addition to this, drone and counter-drone technology is being invested as part of the national defense effort as foreign actors rely more heavily on the technology. Some of the largest challenges with drone countermeasures have been the size and capabilities of a drone strike. The ultimate question that could drive GSIT’s business would be whether AI-enabled autonomous drones can effectively counter a drone strike. If this is the case, GSIT may be well positioned to realize a niche positioning in a growing market. According to its most recent investor slide deck, the drone & satellite AI chips & enterprise edge data centers market can grow from $6.8b in 2025 to $16.4b by 2030.

The ultimate question that investors must consider is whether GSIT can deliver. The firm had previously voiced supply chain challenges in q1’26 that may have impacted the firm’s ability to fully deliver on customer orders. Though having more demand for one’s technology than supply may not be the worst position to be in, not being able to service the volumes in demand isn’t much better, especially when considering the scale of GSIT and the large market share potential the firm is reaching for. Overall, I believe GSIT will need to invest heavily in the supply chain and deepen its partnerships with chipmakers like Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) in order to impactfully bring its APU to market. Given the significant potential for disrupting the current technology providers, GSIT will need to go all-in on its APU CIM in order to achieve any meaningful technological disruption as management voiced in the press release.

In my opinion, one alternative could be for the technology to be acquired by a major chip designer like Intel, which recently raised roughly $16b through investments by the US Government, Nvidia, and SoftBank Group. Perhaps Intel has the firepower to provide GSIT an offer it cannot refuse to kick-start its Client Compute business after peaking in FY21.

GSI Technology Financial Position

Looking ahead to eq2’26, I’m maintaining my previous financial forecast with revenue coming in at $6.3mm with an adjusted EPS of -$0.07/share. Despite the recent proof of function report by Cornell for GSIT APUs, the market will determine the technology’s feasibility for its functionality and price point.

One factor that may raise concerns is the timing of the shelf offering announced on October 21, 2025, one day following the Cornell Research press release. According to the release, GSIT will raise roughly $50mm through a 1.5mm common stock offering and the sale of pre-funded warrants to purchase nearly 3.5mm common shares, pricing shares at $10/share. The capital raised through the issuances will go towards covering corporate expenses as well as the development of the GSIT APU product line.

Risks Related to GSI Technology

Bull Case

The viability of GSIT APUs is becoming increasingly apparent, potentially driving significant growth in niche markets that may require high-performance compute capacity with low power utilization.

Bear Case

GSIT must prove that it can bring its advanced APU product to market by overcoming the supply chain challenges faced in q1’26 while expanding its go-to-market strategy.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

GSIT shares currently trade at 13.23x price/sales, a significant premium over their historical trading average. Ultimately, I believe shares may experience some pullback given the significant run-up following the APU research update. In my view, the press release moved GSIT into a growth tech premium stock, requiring the company to deliver on the necessary growth and product adoption in order for operations to normalize the price premium, or face significant share price decline.

Given the historical risks associated with GSIT’s supply chain and the niche markets its APUs are set to compete in, I am recommending GSIT shares with a Sell rating with a price target of $4.33/share at 4.05x eFY27 price/sales, roughly the midcycle price premium.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: Nebius Pullback: The Smart Money Entry Point
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Nebius (NASDAQ:NBIS) sits at the turning point of a trillion-dollar shift in global cloud infrastructure. With hyperscalers like Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) hitting power and compliance limits, Nebius' sovereign-first foundation, along with its $17.4 billion historical deal with Microsoft, is quietly reshaping compute distribution in the era of AI. Its AI Cloud 3.0 marks a shift from renting GPUs to building a full technology stack, aiming to capture higher-margin, long-term software revenue.

Institutional capital started cycling back since dark-pool inflows and options data confirm new confidence. After a 30% pullback from its $141 high last month, Nebius presents a balanced high-risk, high-reward opportunity for investors ahead of its next potential re-rating cycle. Nebius remains a strong buy as a new emerging sovereign foundation of global AI infrastructure.

The End of Centralization: How Neoclouds Are Rewriting the AI Infrastructure Map

For over a decade, the hyperscaler formula, designed for global scale, dominated cloud computing. But the emerging world of generative AI has shattered that formula. Such compute- and power-hungry models as GPT-5 and Gemini no longer allow for older, centralized architectures to scale. Outrageous power expenses, rigid data-residency regulations, and latency constraints have made it economically imprudent to scale classic hyperscale data centers across international borders.

A new wave of “neoclouds” has emerged. Local, efficient providers that offer secure data control, fast performance, and the same reliability as major hyperscalers. Last year, the neocloud market revenue grew 54% and will compound at over 45% annually from 2024 to 2030, Mordor Intelligence estimates.

The Option Market Spoke: Institutional Buying Following the Retract

The 30% correction from Nebius’ September highs raised questions about whether its fundamentals still supported the earlier rally. Options and dark-pool data, however, point to signs of renewed accumulation.

Institutional investors seem to be setting up for the upside of Nebius, where intense call-option buying hints at fresh confidence. Most active range falls within the $90 and $110 strikes, where the biggest funds are accommodated at $11.7 million open interest at the latter and another $8.5 million at the earlier. The $50 strike activity reflects deep-in-the-money synthetic long positions, a strategy that mirrors stock ownership while requiring less capital. Total open call contracts are higher above 420,000, while the bear puts contracted at 253,000, an indication those are unmistakably institutional confidence and not speculation.

Whalestream reports that approximately $100 million of net inflows were concentrated within the $103.90 area, indicating fresh institutional interest. This was confirmed by Fintel data (extracted from Nasdaq, FINRA, and Capital IQ), which records the off-exchange short-volume ratio near 19%, and reinforces the thesis of significant Nebius buying through dark pools by the institutions. In practice, this supports the view that much of the Nebius buying is happening quietly through dark pools, as aligned with the observed build-up of call options.

In short, as the retail frenzy faded from the post-summer bubble pop, smart money appears to be entering back in the same place where the risk-adjusted gain is highest. This isn’t speculative money; it’s backed by improving fundamentals.

Nebius' Strategy Alignment: From Local Expert to Microsoft's Sovereign Back Booster

When Microsoft signed its five-year $17.4 billion agreement with Nebius, up to $19.4 billion with expansion, it did more than compute outsourcing. It remapped its supply chain. Nebius is going to deliver AI infrastructure for Microsoft's internal large-language-model build as well as its future consumer AI assistant, basically becoming part of Microsoft's compute fabric extension.

This agreement provides Nebius with twin strategic benefits, with multi-year cash visibility and credibility baked into the hyperscale landscape. With most small players bogged down in grid access and permitting, Nebius' sites in Finland, Serbia, and Israel addressed AI infrastructure’s challenges of energy reliability and latency. With locations in renewable-friendly jurisdictions and proprietary energy-management technology, Nebius is already able to price long-term GPU rents lower than peers like CoreWeave (CRWV).

Lastly, the Nebius AI Cloud 3.0 “Aether” launch represents a strategic turn from capacity leasing to orchestration. With the enterprise security standard, granular IAM, native observability, and policy-based governance, regulated enterprises can deploy and manage scale production AI workloads with Aether. Therefore, Nebius' pivot to a full-stack enterprise platform, evident in its Aether 3.0 launch, positions it as the sovereign substitute to AWS Bedrock or Azure OpenAI Service.

The Sector Characterized with Constraints rather than Competition

The least-recognized aspect of the neocloud market is that its expansion stems from constraints rather than competition. There's significantly more demand for regionalized, GPU-dense infrastructure than supply in the marketplace. Supply-chain unreliability in advanced-node chips, -4.1% effect on CAGR per Mordor Intelligence, is still the largest bottleneck, followed by cross-border localizations (-3.2%) and increased green-datacenter capex (-2.3%).

These supply constraints give early movers like Nebius a real advantage in securing power, hardware, and regulatory approvals. Nebius, through advance allocation of capacity within low-cost, energy-rich jurisdictions like Finland and Serbia, has literally secured the scarce inputs that others would find hard to procure.

Just as important, Nebius’ sovereign-compliant design positions it well to benefit from coming regulatory changes. More than 45 nations are projected to implement sovereignty-based data restrictions by late 2025 or early 2026, representing an estimated $8.2 billion in addressable opportunity by 2030. Unlike hyperscalers that must retrofit compliance across sprawling global systems, Nebius was purpose-built for sovereignty from day one.

Risk Assessment: Pains of Scale Under the Growth Narrative

Though execution is robust, Nebius is subject to the usual scaling ills of an early AI-infrastructure pioneer. GPU supply is tight, with continued demand for Nvidia's H100 and upcoming Blackwell processors leading availability by a wide margin, limiting near-term capacity increases. In response, Nebius aims to bring 220 megawatts of powered-up capacity on-line through 2025 year-end, half already on-line, with 1 GW capacity through 2026 end-date across new data center construction in Finland, New Jersey, Britain, and Israel. Its fast-paced buildout underlies its elevated annualized run-rate revenue forecast of $900 million to $1.1 billion and lays the groundwork for a mid-term multi-billion-dollar revenue possibility.

While Nebius still relies on large strategic contracts, its growing partnerships with companies like Cloudflare, Shopify, and Prosus are helping diversify revenue sources. Increasing green data center capex and regulatory complexity across Europe and the Middle East may erode margins, but Nebius’ renewable-powered sites and sovereign-compliant design insulate much of that risk. Management expects Nebius to come out with positive adjusted EBITDA in 2026, a sign of disciplined expansion in a market experiencing temporary, not structural, issues.

Takeaway

Nebius is turning out to be a major beneficiary of the trillion-dollar neocloud revolution. Its $17.4 billion Microsoft partnership, sovereign-first strategy, and rapid Aether 3.0 expansion provide multi-year visibility. With institutional investors returning after a 30% correction, Nebius offers meaningful upside as a foundational player in the next phase of global AI expansion.
